perturbation.original_text: (a) For the period beginning on the Effective Date and ending on the * anniversary thereof (the "Restriction Period"), neither Selling Parties nor any subsidiary, sister entity, or parent will, directly or indirectly, either as a partner or owner or in any other capacity: (i) engage in the homebuilding or home sales business within 100 miles of the Las Vegas metropolitan area (a "Competing Business");
perturbation.changed_text: (a) For the period beginning on the Effective Date and ending on the * anniversary thereof (the "Restriction Period"), neither Selling Parties nor any subsidiary, sister entity, or parent will, directly or indirectly, either as a partner or owner or in any other capacity: (i) engage in any business within 100 miles of the Las Vegas metropolitan area (a "Competing Business");
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts competition to the 'homebuilding or home sales business.' The modified text broadens this to 'any business,' which is overly broad and potentially unenforceable under Nevada law regarding non-compete agreements. This lack of specificity creates uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 613.195 requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration. Broadening the restriction to 'any business' makes the agreement unreasonable and likely unenforceable under Nevada law, contradicting the statute's intent to protect fair competition and employee mobility.

perturbation.justification: The original text restricts competition to 'homebuilding or home sales business,' while the changed text broadens it to 'any business.' This change contradicts NRS 613.195, as cited in the Law Explanation, which requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope. The modified text is overly broad, making the agreement unreasonable and likely unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes 'NRS 613.195 Noncompetition covenants: Limitations; enforceability; revision by court; award to prevailing party,' directly referencing the relevant statute and reinforcing the contradiction because the change now violates the limitations of non-compete agreements, making it less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (ii) recruit, hire or discuss employment for a Competing Business with any person who is, or within the six month period preceding the date of such activity was, an employee of the Company or Meritage (other than as a result of a general solicitation for employment); or
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) recruit, hire or discuss employment for any business with any person who is, or within the six month period preceding the date of such activity was, an employee of the Company or Meritage (other than as a result of a general solicitation for employment); or
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits restrictions to recruiting for a 'Competing Business.' The modified text extends it to recruiting for 'any business.' This is a substantial expansion of the non-compete clause that creates uncertainty about whether it is reasonable under Nevada law.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 613.195 requires that non-compete agreements be reasonable. This section makes the agreement overly broad in the kind of company that the individual is unable to recruit for, rendering the contract potentially unenforceable under Nevada law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the scope of the non-compete agreement from 'Competing Business' to 'any business'. The Law Explanation states that NRS 613.195 requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable, and the change makes the agreement overly broad, rendering it potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'NRS 613.195 Noncompetition covenants: Limitations; enforceability; revision by court; award to prevailing party.' This supports the claim that the altered text may contradict Nevada law regarding the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, because extending the restriction to 'any business' could be deemed unreasonable and thus create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's trade secrets and all other confidential and proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature, including without limitation, proprietary information of the Company, as it exists from time to time (collectively, "Confidential Information"), are valuable and unique assets of the Company and therefore agree that, during the Restriction Period, except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed, they will not, and will use their best efforts to ensure that their directors, officers, employees, advisers, agents and consultants do not, disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless previously authorized in writing to do so by Meritage.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's information, including customer lists (collectively, "Confidential Information"), is valuable and therefore agree that, during the Restriction Period, they will not disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless previously authorized in writing to do so by Meritage.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly to include 'trade secrets and all other confidential and proprietary information.' The modified text narrows the definition to 'customer lists,' excluding other potentially protected information. This limited definition weakens the protection of confidential information that could be protected under Nevada's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 600A.030 defines trade secrets much more broadly than simply 'customer lists.' By narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the modified contract fails to protect the full scope of trade secrets as defined by Nevada law, potentially undermining the protections afforded by the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS: CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) [Rev. 6/29/2024 4:37:37 PM--2023] CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) NRS 600A.010 Short title. NRS 600A.030 Definitions. NRS 600A.032 Presumption of reasonable effort by owner to maintain secrecy; rebuttal of presumption. NRS 600A.035 Theft of trade secrets prohibited; criminal penalties. NRS 600A.040 Actual or threatened misappropriation: Injunctive relief. NRS 600A.050 Misappropriation: Damages. NRS 600A.055 Misappropriation and posting or dissemination on Internet: Effect. NRS 600A.060 Attorneys fees. NRS 600A.070 Preservation of secrecy. NRS 600A.080 Limitation of actions. NRS 600A.090 Effect of chapter on other law and remedies. NRS 600A.100 Inapplicability of chapter to acts which occurred before, or began before and continue after, July 1, 1987. _________ NRS 600A.010 Short title. This chapter may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added to NRS by 1987, 19 ) NRS 600A.030 Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. Improper means includes, without limitation: (a) Theft; (b) Bribery; (c) Misrepresentation; (d) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy; (e) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty imposed by common law, statute, contract, license, protective order or other court or administrative order; and (f) Espionage through electronic or other means. 2. Misappropriation means: (a) Acquisition of the trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (1) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (2) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (I) Derived from or through a person who had used improper means to acquire it; (II) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text narrows the definition of "Confidential Information" from a broad scope including trade secrets and proprietary information to just 'customer lists.' This contradicts NRS 600A.030, which defines 'Improper means' related to trade secrets to include 'willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy,' and 'Misappropriation' related to trade secrets. By limiting the definition, the modified contract undermines the protection of trade secrets as defined by Nevada law, potentially failing to maintain the legally required secrecy. The original contract protected a wider range of confidential information, whereas the modified version weakens this protection, directly contradicting the intent of NRS 600A regarding trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a court of competent jurisdiction determines that the Restriction Period is unenforceable, the Restriction Period shall mean *. Additionally, if any provision of this Agreement is held to be illegal, invalid or unenforceable under any applicable law, then such provision will be deemed to be modified to the minimum extent necessary to render it legal, valid and enforceable, and if no such modification will render it legal, valid and enforceable, then this Agreement will be construed as if not containing the provision held to be invalid, and the rights and obligations of the parties will be construed and enforced accordingly.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a court determines the Restriction Period is unenforceable, the Restriction Period shall mean *. Additionally, if any provision of this Agreement is held to be illegal, invalid or unenforceable under any applicable law, then this Agreement will be construed as if not containing the provision held to be invalid, and the rights and obligations of the parties will be construed and enforced accordingly.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes a severability clause that allows the agreement to be modified to the minimum extent possible to be legal, valid, and enforceable, which attempts to preserve the agreement. The modified text removes this provision. By removing it, the agreement is made much less flexible and much more likely to be challenged in court, in opposition to contract law, which allows parties to have the ability to attempt to preserve their contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184 states that if part of an agreement is unenforceable, a court may still enforce the rest of the agreement, if it is legal, and if the performance is not changed. By removing the severability clause, this part of contract law is directly contradicted, decreasing the enforceability of the overall agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Restatement (Second) of Contracts - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Widely cited American legal treatise The Restatement (Second) of the Law of Contracts is a legal treatise from the second series of the Restatements of the Law , and seeks to inform judges and lawyers about general principles of contract common law. It is one of the best-recognized and frequently cited legal treatises [ 1 ] in all of American jurisprudence . Every first-year law student in the United States is exposed to it, and it is a frequently cited non-binding authority in all of U.S. common law in the areas of contracts and commercial transactions. [ 2 ] The American Law Institute began work on the second edition in 1962 and completed it in 1979; the version in use at present has a copyright year of 1981. [ 3 ] For an explanation of the purpose of a restatement of law, see Restatement of the Law . Use and commentary [ edit ] Legal scholars and jurists have commented extensively on the Restatement, both in contrasting it with aspects of the first Restatement, and in evaluating its influence and effectiveness in reaching its stated objectives. It is in this context of direct review that one can find numerous arguments both favoring and criticizing some aspects of the Restatement as an independent source of legal scholarship. [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] Although several sections of the Restatement contained new rules which sometimes contradicted existing law, courts citing these sections have predominantly adopted the Restatement's view, citing them as a court would cite statute or code. [ 9 ] Far more common, however, is the practice of citing the Restatement to clarify generally accepted doctrine in every major area of contract and commercial law. It is in this context of legal research that one can find the Restatement used as direct substantiation and persuasive authority , to validate the arguments and interpretations of individual legal practitioners. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Legacy [ edit ] Although the Restatement of Contracts is still an influential academic work, certain aspects have been superseded in everyday legal practice by the Uniform Commercial Code . Specifically, the UCC has replaced the Restatement (Second) of Contracts in regard to the sale of goods. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts remains the unofficial authority for aspects of contract law which find their genesis in the common law principles of the United States and, previously, England. [ citation needed ] See also [ edit ] Arthur Linton Corbin , first reporter Corpus Juris Secundum References [ edit ] ^ Acceptable citation format under the Bluebook : "Restatement (Second) of Contracts § ___ (Am. L. Inst. 1981)." The Bluebook: A Uniform System of Citation § 12.8.5 (Columbia Law Review Ass'n et al. eds., 17th ed. 2000) ^ Bauman, Richard (2002). Ideology and Community in the First Wave of Critical Legal Studies . Toronto: University of Toronto Press. ISBN 0-8020-8341-2 . ^ Beale, Hugh (2002). Contract Law: Casebooks for the Common Law of Europe . Oxford: Hart Pub. ISBN 978-1-84113-237-2 . ^ Interpretation and Legal Effect in the Second "Restatement of Contracts" Robert Braucher Columbia Law Review , Vol. 81, No. 1 (Jan., 1981), pp. 13-18 ^ Jacobs, Legal Realism or Legal Fiction? Impracticability Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts , 87 Com. L.J. 289 (1982). ^ Braucher, Offer and Acceptance in the Second Restatement , 74 Yale L.J. 302 (1964). ^ Kelly, The Codification of Contract Law in the Twentieth Century , 88 Dick. L. Rev. 289 (1984). ^ Keyes, The Restatement (Second): Its Misleading Quality and a Proposal for Its Amelioration , 13 Pepp. L. Rev. 23 (1985). ^ Gregory E. Maggs, Ipse Dixit: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts and the Modern Development of Contract Law , 66 Geo. Wash. L. Rev. 508 (1998). ^ Restitution in the Second "Restatement of Contracts"Joseph M. Perillo Columbia Law Review , Vol. 81, No. 1 (Jan., 1981), pp. 37-51 ^ Contracts:
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the severability clause, which is a mechanism to preserve the contract's validity by modifying unenforceable provisions to the minimum extent necessary. By removing this, the contract directly contradicts the principle described in the LLM's Law Explanation, which references Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by describing the Restatement as a widely cited legal treatise that seeks to inform judges and lawyers about general principles of contract common law. Removing the severability clause makes the entire agreement more vulnerable to being deemed unenforceable, and thus contradicts contract law, where severability allows the agreement to attempt to continue and remain valid.
